title: Concurrency Budgets, Backpressure, Rate Limits, and Worker Threads · Compiler Case: Event Loop Lag Blocking
summary: The service launches unbounded promises, blocks the event loop with CPU work, uses one global rate counter, buffers queues without rejection, and cannot explain overload recovery. Planned artifact: Add and verify the httpsts event loop lag blocking increment in admission, concurrency, queue, rate, and CPU-work budget. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish asynchronous concurrency from parallelism and measure CPU blocking, synchronous I/O, long callbacks, and event-loop delay.
Own background promise lifetimes, observe rejection and multiple-resolution failures, fail safely, and distinguish crash recovery from request error handling.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Node and Express server Predict Event Loop Lag Blocking · Theory Event Loop Lag Blocking
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory team handles case 4689 in a signed fulfillment webhook gateway under replay pressure. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to combine concurrency, queue, tenant-rate, and bounded CPU-offload decisions; show recovery after the overload input is removed; then rotate a signing key while an old valid session remains within policy. Browser code models pure decisions only. Node, Express, listener, TLS, PostgreSQL, proxy, worker, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Event Loop Lag Blocking contract for the supplied data.

Evidence target: Node and Express server Predict Event Loop Lag Blocking · Theory Event Loop Lag Blocking.
